normalized_episode: Value of Treemap Visualization in Analysis Workspace Value of Treemap Visualization Treemaps display hierarchical data as a set of nested rectangles in which the rectangles range in size from top left (largest) to bottom right (smallest). Use cases for Treemap visualizations include: Showing the proportions between each part and the whole. Showing the pattern of the measure distribution across each level of categories in the hierarchy. Showing attributes using size and color coding. Spotting patterns, outliers, most-important contributors, and exceptions. In Adobe Analytics, the smaller boxes may not have text to label the box; you must mouse over them to view the dimension item and the associated details.  When to Use Treemap Visualization While the bar stacked visualization can also show proportions between each part and the whole, similar to the Treemap visualization, these two display values differently. The bar-stacked visualization will visualize the highlighted dimension values in each column and compare the results. For instance, if "Product A, Product B, Product C, and Product D" are the most viewed products for Column A, but these products are not the top viewed products shown in Column B, the bar stacked visualization will compare the two columns and base the analysis on the column that is performing better. As a result, we can only understand a little about Column B's top-performing products. In certain scenarios, we want to know how a group of products is performing independently while still being able to view them in a single chart, which is an ideal time to use a Treemap visualization. Creating Treemap Visualization Based on the above Freeform table, we see that: The top products driving product views for New Visitors are: Breville Barista Express Espresso Machine (33,595 product views) Crafthouse by Fortes Cocktail Smoking Box (24,036 product views) Breville Barista Touch Espresso Machine (17,210 product views) Le Creuset Signature Cast-Iron Round Dutch Oven (15,889 product views) Le Creuset Signature Cast-Iron Round Dutch Oven, 6 3/4-qt (13,086 product views) The top products driving product views for Repeat Visitors are: Crafthouse by Fortes Cocktail Smoking Box (12,439 product views) Breville Barista Touch Espresso Machine (11,013 product views) Le Creuset Signature Cast-Iron Round Dutch Oven (9,195 product views) Le Creuset Signature Cast-Iron Round Dutch Oven, 6 3/4-qt (7,761 product views) Breville Barista Touch Espresso Machine (7,712 product views) As shown below, we are able to understand how the five products perform for New Visitors and Repeat Visitors in one visualization and use the size of the boxes to compare the product performance: You can also click on the gear to turn the numbers into percentages to more easily compare the two columns:   Value of Treemap Visualization Treemaps display hierarchical data as a set of nested rectangles in which the rectangles range in size from top left (largest) to bottom right (smallest). normalized_episode: However, defining objectives at the outset allows for more targeted analysis that effectively addresses business needs. This organized approach ensures that CJA is used to its full potential, generating actionable insights to drive business results. Establishing clear analysis objectives is a fundamental step in using CJA effectively. These objectives guide your entire analysis process, providing direction and focus. Whether your goal is to understand customer interactions, identify points of customer disengagement, or explore personalization opportunities, your objectives will define how you approach your data and use CJA. By starting with well-defined objectives, you pave the way for focused and effective analysis. 2. Validate Your Analysis Data accuracy is crucial in all forms of data analysis, including CJA. To derive meaningful and reliable insights, it's essential to confirm the integrity of your data in relation to your analysis goals before proceeding with any interpretation. Suppose a retail company operates both online and physical stores and wants to understand the behavior of customers who use both platforms. They can use a filter-based cross-check to validate their population for analysis. First, filters are applied to separate customers who made purchases exclusively online from those who shopped only in-store, ensuring no overlap between these groups. Once the filters are applied, the groups are cross-checked to validate that there are no overlaps. This way, we ensure that customers are not counted in both the online and in-store groups. Then, a high-level data filter check is conducted for online and physical store customers as a validation measure. This step confirms that the total number of unique customers in all groups matches the total in the provider source database, ensuring that all customers are accounted for and the data is accurate. By following these steps, the company can confidently proceed with its CJA, knowing their analysis is based on valid data. This validation process is crucial in understanding customer behavior across different platforms and optimizing marketing and sales strategies. By investing time in data validation, you establish a robust foundation for your CJA analysis, increasing the likelihood of generating accurate insights, making informed decisions, and achieving your data goals. 3. Use Visualizations CJA Data visualizations serve as a powerful tool in understanding the customer journey by bringing clarity and insight against complex data sets. They enable us to identify and highlight key trends or patterns in a visually appealing and easy-to-understand format. CJA provides an array of visualization options that can greatly enhance your data analysis process. By leveraging these visualization options, you can present information in a way that is not only more comprehensible but also more actionable. CJA also provides a forecasting feature within Analysis Workspace. This feature can be used against a standard or calculated metric with any supported time granularity, which includes hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly. Forecasts can be displayed in a time-series Freeform Table. If ‘Show forecast’ is enabled for the Freeform Table in user preferences, the forecasting will automatically appear for the first metric column added to the table. 4. Iterate and Refine Your Analysis The customer journey, comprising various interactions and experiences with your platforms, is a dynamic, ever-evolving process. It can fluctuate based on numerous factors. Therefore, regular and comprehensive CJA analysis is vital for understanding your customers' needs, preferences, and expectations more deeply. This understanding is not a one-off achievement but requires consistent and thorough analysis of your customer journeys. Your CJA efforts should keep up with the constantly changing customer behavior, the impact of new marketing campaigns, and the various paths customers may take. By consistently revising and refining your analysis, you ensure that your business strategies remain relevant and effective in meeting your customers' changing needs. This commitment to continuous improvement and adaptation, to staying flexible and responsive, is key to maintaining ongoing effectiveness with your CJA analysis efforts. 5. Share Insights Across Your Organization After analyzing the insights from your CJA work, it's crucial to share this knowledge across your entire organization. This approach goes beyond merely sharing information; it aims to create a comprehensive understanding of the customer's journey across various platforms and experiences. By using the same set of data and insights, everyone can share a unified vision and understanding of business performance. In turn, this shared understanding helps to further strengthen the organization's ability to make data-informed decisions. Not only does this approach ensure that everyone is working towards the same objectives, but it also enables the organization to make decisions that are in the best interest of both the customer and the business. Ultimately, by fostering a culture of shared understanding and alignment around CJA insights, you empower your organization to create more meaningful and impactful customer experiences. Conclusion In conclusion, effectively using CJA can be likened to a successful treasure hunt. By adhering to best practices such as setting clear objectives, validating your analysis, using visualizations, refining your analysis iteratively, and sharing insights across your organization, you're effectively employing your strategic compass and detailed map. This proactive approach aligns with the saying "an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure". By setting clear goals and validating your data, you're deciphering the intricate clues of your customer's journey. Similarly, refining your analysis and sharing insights ensures that you don't lose sight of your ultimate goal distributing valuable insights into your customer journey. It's important to remember that the customer journey isn't static; it evolves like the path in a treasure hunt, requiring regular analysis and refinement. Ultimately, it results in the treasure of understanding and catering to your customers' needs, driving growth, and achieving successful business outcomes.

normalized_episode: Tagging some people for discussion: @junlincc @rusackas @mihir174 @jess-dillard @kgabryje @graceguo-supercat I like this idea, but wonder if it becomes confusing in instances where individual charts in a dashboard have different filters applied to them. If a user sees the filter summary bar, they will likely assume all charts below the summary have those filters applied, which may not be the case. We would need one of these for every chart, which brings us close to the solution we have now. > I like this idea, but wonder if it becomes confusing in instances where individual charts in a dashboard have different filters applied to them. If a user sees the filter summary bar, they will likely assume all charts below the summary have those filters applied, which may not be the case. We would need one of these for every chart, which brings us close to the solution we have now. 1. Filter summary bar is optionally to use in every dashboard 2. charts already have an individual filter indicator to show applied filters 3. it is up to the dashboard creator to arrange charts in a make sense layout and deciding to use the Summary bar is a matter of a choice according to your use-case The advantages that I see with respect to this bar are the following, according to me: * Shows the context right there on the screen without having to do viz triangle tooltip hover over. * Allows usage of more real estate (the vertical filter bar doesn't need to be always ON to see the filter context) * It uses some real estate as a row, but it is very minimal compared to the vertical native filters control pane * If the number filters are too high, then this may not make sense. But, the fact that this is optional, makes it easy to choose and enable whenever this makes sense. I assume you want user to use **either** filter indicator **or** filter bar. But their functionalities are not fully matched if just looking at current description: - as @jess-dillard said, not all charts in the tab have the same applicable filters. - user click filter indicator can focus the filter component - filter indicator shows all filters that can apply to this chart, which includes filter has values and filters without values. - Do you have design for dashboard with nested tabs? the filter summary might show duplicated places. > I assume you want user to use **either** filter indicator **or** filter bar. But their functionalities are not fully matched if just looking at current description: > > * as @jess-dillard said, not all charts in the tab have the same applicable filters. > * user click filter indicator can focus the filter component > * filter indicator shows all filters that can apply to this chart, which includes filter has values and filters without values. > * Do you have design for dashboard with nested tabs? the filter summary might show duplicated places. thanks for the input, I've updated the description . basically as you indicated, chart filter indicator is not useful in our usecase and we plan not to use it, but instead use the Filter summary bar for getting a quick context on what data the existing charts are displaying without really analysing filters into depth > chart filter indicator is not useful in our usecase and we plan not to use it, but instead use the Filter summary bar for getting a quick context on what data the existing charts are displaying without really analysing filters into depth @amitmiran137 Can't this strategy produce wrong conclusions? I can see two examples of this: 1 - We could have two different datasets that contain the same field name City. A user creates only one filter named City for dataset 1. We have two charts, one from each dataset, and both charts can be filtered by City (different fields). When displaying the filter summary, the user will think both charts are filtered by City but in reality, only the chart that belongs to dataset 1 will be filtered. 2 - A chart contains an incompatible filter. When displaying the filter summary, the user will think the chart is filtered when it's not, leading to wrong conclusions. Perhaps it would be possible to have something like this: - when not hovering a chart, it shows all currently set filters, irrespective of whether or not they apply to any/some/all charts - when hovering a chart, it shows only the filters applied to the current chart > * when not hovering a chart, it shows all currently set filters, irrespective of whether or not they apply to any/some/all charts If we do something like this, we would need to display a tooltip or text to let the user know that they can hover and that the summary is not accurate when not hovering. > * when hovering a chart, it shows only the filters applied to the current chart Wouldn't this practically replace/duplicate the filter indicators? In airbnb when we design Filter Indicator, **`hover` solution was discussed and rejected**! Because when user looking at a dashboard, they hate to hove chart one by one to see which filter are applied.

normalized_episode: Hi @Anakeen94 As a test you may try to import your Google Ads data into windsor.ai account and export it into BigQuery. windsor.ai Google Ads.png 829×369 46.9 KB Hi @Anakeen94 It looks like you’re experiencing a common issue where the data from Google Ads and BigQuery doesn’t perfectly align, especially when it comes to conversions. There are a few potential reasons for this discrepancy: Different Timezones : Check if the time zones in Google Ads and BigQuery are aligned. Sometimes the data discrepancies come from data being reported at different times of the day based on timezone settings. Conversion Attribution Models : Google Ads may use different attribution models (e.g., last-click, data-driven) while BigQuery could be reflecting different conversion types or time frames. Ensure that the attribution settings match between both platforms. Data Refresh Rates : BigQuery Data Transfer might not update in real-time and could lag behind Google Ads by a day or more. Ensure you’re checking data from the same time period and accounting for potential data refresh lags. Custom Columns or Filters : Ensure that the fields you’re querying in BigQuery are the same as the ones you’re looking at in Google Ads. It’s possible that filters, conversion categories, or even custom metrics set up in Google Ads are not reflected in your BigQuery data schema. If you’ve checked the above and the discrepancy persists, you may want to explore using a different data connector like Windsor.ai, which offers advanced and more granular control over the data extraction and transfer from Google Ads to BigQuery. Hope this helps!

normalized_episode: Compare Current Day with the last 7 days average Hi I’m trying to build a look/table which shows a measure for current day value, as well as an average of the last 7 days. The issue is this measure is a “number” type cuz it’s a calculation of division. So I’m having trouble creating this measure with filters of date_day_date. Is there any other ways to have the same measure but applied with different time frame/filter in the same look/dashboard? Thanks! Hi I’m trying to build a look/table which shows a measure for current day value, as well as an average of the last 7 days. The issue is this measure is a “number” type cuz it’s a calculation of division. So I’m having trouble creating this measure with filters of date_day_date. Is there any other ways to have the same measure but applied with different time frame/filter in the same look/dashboard? Thanks! I think you can achieve your goal by creating two custom fields along with a date filter on your explore. Suppose you have a measure that represents the value per day. In my example - Count. To create current day value, you can create a Custom measure with the following custom filter : ${history.created_date} > add_days(-1,now()) To create Last 7 days Average, you can create a Table calculation with the following Custom Expression: round(${history.count}/7,1) Please filter the explorer with “is in the past 7 days” and it should show the values you wanted to see.

normalized_episode: Customer Journey Report Help Hi @laishasaba , Thanks for reaching out to the Community, I believe a lot of users have this question 🙂 I understand you're seeing a discrepancy between the metrics in your Ads analytics and your Journey report, right? In the customer journey report, the creation of a contact record comes first, followed by an ad interaction. Therefore, we are only looking at existing contacts who have subsequently interacted with an ad. For instance, if we analyze a particular contact, and find that the ad interaction occurred before the contact was created, that contact will not be included in the customer journey report. They will, however, show up in the Ads analytics data. I hope this helps clarify, Diana Hi @laishasaba , Thanks for reaching out to the Community, I believe a lot of users have this question 🙂 I understand you're seeing a discrepancy between the metrics in your Ads analytics and your Journey report, right? In the customer journey report, the creation of a contact record comes first, followed by an ad interaction. Therefore, we are only looking at existing contacts who have subsequently interacted with an ad. For instance, if we analyze a particular contact, and find that the ad interaction occurred before the contact was created, that contact will not be included in the customer journey report. They will, however, show up in the Ads analytics data. I hope this helps clarify, Diana
